original_abstract: This article explores the transformative role of the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians in engendering higher theological education in Africa. Grounded in African Women’s theology, the study analyses initiatives in championing gender equality, inclusivity, and intersectional solidarity within theological academia. The study highlights the historic journey of the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians in promoting the integration of gender-sensitive curricular and nurturing a new generation of African women theologians. Methodologically, I adopt a qualitative design and use African Women’s Theology as a lens to understand the transformative strides made by the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians towards engendering theological higher education in Africa.
humanized_abstract: Summary This study examines the contribution of the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians to the engendering of higher theological education in Africa. The study is framed in the African Women’s Theology paradigm. It examines the project of promoting gender justice, inclusivity and solidarity with marginalized groups in the context of theological education. In a critical reflection, the study narrates the Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians’ involvement and efforts towards developing a gender responsive curriculum and equipping the African woman theologian for the challenges in higher theological education in Africa. Based on this background, the study adopted qualitative methodology and used the prisms of African Women’s Theology as a hermeneutic lens for examining the contributions of Circle of Concerned African Women Theologians towards the engendering of higher theological education in Africa.
